questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question_text:
Refer to the following information of phosphorus (P) and chlorine (Cl).
PCl
Atomic number1517
Relative atomic mass31.035.5

sub_questions.label: a
sub_questions.question_text: State the electronic arrangement of a phosphorus atom.

sub_questions.label: b
sub_questions.question_text: All chlorine atoms have the same atomic number. Explain why some chlorine atoms have different mass numbers.
sub_questions.answer: Chlorine exists as isotopes. / There are chlorine atoms with same number of protons but different number of neutrons.

sub_questions.label: c
sub_questions.question_text: A compound of phosphorus and chlorine has a relative molecular mass smaller than 250. It contains 22.6% of phosphorus by mass.

children.label: i
children.question_text: Deduce the molecular formula of the compound.
children.answer:
Number of moles of P : Number of moles of Cl = 0.226/31.0 : 0.774/35.5 = 1 : 3
Molecular formula is  \( (\mathrm{PCl}_{3})_{n} \)
\( (31.0 + 35.5 \times 3) n < 250 \)
n = 1
Molecular formula is  \( PCl_{3} \)

children.label: ii
children.question_text: Draw the electron diagram for the compound, showing electrons in the outermost shells only.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question_text:
The set-up of an experiment for studying the movement of ions is shown below.

[IMG:q2_img_0.jpg]

sub_questions.label: a
sub_questions.question_text: Explain why the filter paper is soaked with  \( K_{2}SO_{4}(aq) \)  instead of water.
sub_questions.answer: To increase the electrical conductivity of the filter paper / To increase the number of mobile ions / To provide mobile ions

sub_questions.label: b
sub_questions.question_text: State the colour of  \( FeSO_{4}(aq) \) .
sub_questions.answer: pale green

sub_questions.label: c
sub_questions.question_text: Explain what would be observed around the middle of the filter paper when the circuit is closed for a period of time.
sub_questions.answer:
Blue colour appears around the middle of the filter paper.
\( Fe^{2+}(aq) \)  ions move towards negative pole and  \( \mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_{6}^{3-}(aq) \)  ions move towards positive pole forming a blue compound.

sub_questions.label: d
sub_questions.question_text: The experiment is repeated, but the two poles of the cell have been reversed at the very beginning. Explain what would be observed around the middle of the filter paper when the circuit is closed for aperiod of time.
sub_questions.answer:
The colour around the middle of the filter paper remains unchanged.
\( Fe^{2+}(aq) \)  ions and  \( \mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_{6}^{3-}(aq) \text{ ions do not migrate towards each other. } / \mathrm{K}^{+}(aq) \text{ ions and } \mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-}(aq) \text{ ions migrate towards each other but do not form coloured compounds.} \)

questions.subject: Chemistry
topics: Topic 5: Fossil Fuels and Carbon Compounds
questions.question_text: The diagram below shows an experimental set-up in which the glass wool soaked with octane is heated occasionally and the broken unglazed porcelain is heated strongly. Some gases are collected in the test tube over water.

sub_questions.label: a
sub_questions.question_text: Name the type of reaction that occurs in the boiling tube. Suggest one importance of this type of reaction in industry.
sub_questions.answer:
cracking 
To produce alkenes / To produce smaller hydrocarbons from larger hydrocarbons / To convert heavy oil to petrol

sub_questions.label: b
sub_questions.question_text: Explain why, instead of a large piece of unglazed porcelain, broken unglazed porcelain is used in this experiment.
sub_questions.answer: The reaction will be faster when using broken unglazed porcelain instead of a large piece of unglazed porcelain due to larger surface area.

sub_questions.label: c
sub_questions.question_text: Suppose that during the experiment, octane changes to ethane gas and propene gas only and they can be collected in the test tube.

children.label: i
children.question_text: Write the balanced equation for the reaction of changing octane to ethane and propene.

children.label: ii
children.question_text: The gases collected in the test tube are shaken throughly with a few drops of \(Br_{2}(in\ CH_{3}CCl_{3})\) solution.

children.label: a
children.question_text: State the expected observation.
children.answer: Orange / brown \(Br_{2}\) solution turns to colourless.

children.label: b
children.question_text: Draw the structure of the product formed from the reaction between propene and \(Br_{2}\).

sub_questions.label: d
sub_questions.question_text: When no more gas can be collected, what should be done to end the experiment for safety consideration? Explain your answer.
sub_questions.answer: The delivery tube should be taken out of the water level before removing the heating source, otherwise sucking back will happen / the boiling tube will be cracked.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question_text: Consider the molecules  \( CO_{2} \) ,  \( CS_{2} \)  and  \( CH_{2}Br_{2} \) .

sub_questions.label: a
sub_questions.question_text: For each of the following molecules, draw its three-dimensional structure.

sub_questions.label: b
sub_questions.question_text: Identify, with explanation, the polar bond(s) in  \( CH_{2}Br_{2} \) .
sub_questions.answer:
• C—H and C—Br bonds are polar. 
• C and H / C and Br have different electronegativities. /
C is more electron-withdrawing than H / Br is more electron-withdrawing than C.

sub_questions.label: c
sub_questions.question_text: Suggest why, under room temperature and pressure,  \( CO_{2} \)  is a gas but  \( CS_{2} \)  is a liquid.
sub_questions.answer:
The intermolecular forces between  \( CS_{2}/CO_{2} \)  molecules are van der Waals' forces. 
As  \( CS_{2} \)  has greater molecular size than  \( CO_{2} \) , the van der Waals' forces between  \( CS_{2} \)  molecules are stronger than those between  \( CO_{2} \)  molecules.

questions.subject: Chemistry
topics: Topic 5: Fossil Fuels and Carbon Compounds
questions.question_text:
Polymer B shown below can be used as water absorbing material in diapers. It can be formed from the polymerisation of compound A.

[IMG:q5_img_0.jpg]

sub_questions.label: a
sub_questions.question_text: Draw the structure of compound A and state its systematic name.
sub_questions.answer:
[IMG:ans_q5_a_1.jpg]

propanoic acid

sub_questions.label: b
sub_questions.question_text: State the type of polymerisation for the formation of B from A.
sub_questions.answer: addition

sub_questions.label: c
sub_questions.question_text: Suggest why the relative molecular mass of B is expressed using a range of values instead of a single fixed value.
sub_questions.answer: B is a mixture of polymer molecules with different lengths.

sub_questions.label: d
sub_questions.question_text: It is known that the reaction of polymer B with NaOH(aq) forms polymer C which can absorb water better. Draw the structure of C.
sub_questions.answer: [IMG:q5_img_1.jpg]

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question_text: Citric acid is a tribasic acid found in lemon. It is a white solid and soluble in water.

sub_questions.label: a
sub_questions.question_text:
In the structure of citric acid shown below, circle ALL ionisable hydrogen atom(s) making it a tribasic acid.

[IMG:q6_img_0.jpg]
sub_questions.answer: [IMG:ans_q6_a_0.jpg]

sub_questions.label: b
sub_questions.question_text:
A solid sample contained citric acid and other soluble inert substances. 1.65 g of the sample was dissolved in deionised water and diluted to 250.0 cm³ in apparatus X. After that, 25.00 cm³ of the diluted solution was withdrawn and titrated with 0.123 M NaOH(aq) using phenolphthalein as an indicator. 18.45 cm³ of the NaOH(aq) was required to reach the end point. 
(Molar mass of citric acid = 192.0 g)

children.label: i
children.question_text: What is apparatus X?

children.label: ii
children.question_text: Calculate the percentage by mass of citric acid in the solid sample.
children.answer:
Number of moles of NaOH(aq) = 0.123 x 0.01845
Number of moles of citric acid reacting with NaOH(aq) = 0.123 x 0.01845 + 3
Number of moles of citric acid in the sample = 0.123 x 0.01845 + 3 x 10
Percentage by mass of citric acid in the solid sample
= (0.123 x 0.01845 ÷ 3 x 10 x 192.0 ÷ 1.65) x 100%
= 88.0 %

sub_questions.label: c
sub_questions.question_text: A few drops of lemon juice are added to sodium hydrogencarbonate powder.

children.label: i
children.question_text: State the expected observation.
children.answer: Colourless gas bubbles form. / Effervescence occurs.

children.label: ii
children.question_text: Write the ionic equation for the reaction involved.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question_text: The enthalpy change of formation of  \( \mathrm{MgCO_{3}(s)} \)  can be obtained using an indirect method. Firstly, the enthalpy change for the reaction of  \( \mathrm{MgCO_{3}(s)} \)  with  \( \mathrm{H_{2}SO_{4}(aq)} \) , and that of  \( \mathrm{Mg(s)} \)  with  \( \mathrm{H_{2}SO_{4}(aq)} \)  are respectively determined experimentally. After that, the enthalpy change of formation of  \( \mathrm{MgCO_{3}(s)} \)  is obtained through calculation with given enthalpy changes of formation of  \( \mathrm{CO_{2}(g)} \)  and  \( \mathrm{H_{2}O(l)} \) .

sub_questions.label: a
sub_questions.question_text: According to definition, under which condition could the 'heat change' of a reaction be regarded as the 'enthalpy change'?
sub_questions.answer: constant pressure

sub_questions.label: b
sub_questions.question_text: Explain why, instead of a direct method, an indirect method is used to obtain the enthalpy change of formation of  \( \mathrm{MgCO_{3}(s)} \) .
sub_questions.answer: It is very difficult for Mg(s), C(s) and O_{2}(g) to react directly to form MgCO_{3}(s).

sub_questions.label: c
sub_questions.question_text: In order to determine experimentally the enthalpy change for the reaction of  \( \mathrm{MgCO_{3}(s)}  \)  with  \( \mathrm{H_{2}SO_{4}(aq)} \) , an accurate mass of  \( \mathrm{MgCO_{3}(s)} \)  was firstly allowed to react with excess  \( \mathrm{H_{2}SO_{4}(aq)} \)  in a polystyrene foam cup. The maximum rise in temperature of the mixture was then found. After calculation, the enthalpy change for the reaction can be obtained.

children.label: i
children.question_text: Suggest one possible error for the above experimental procedure.
children.answer: Heat loss to surrounding / PS cup absorbs heat.

children.label: ii
children.question_text: Explain whether the enthalpy change for the reaction of  \( \mathrm{CaCO_{3}(s)} \)  with  \( \mathrm{H_{2}SO_{4}(aq)} \)  can be obtained using a similar experimental procedure.
children.answer: No, because insoluble  \( CaSO_{4} \)  will be formed.

sub_questions.label: d
sub_questions.question_text:
Using the information given below, calculate the standard enthalpy change of formation of  \( MgCO_{3}(s) \) .

Standard enthaply change for the reaction of \(MgCO_{3}(s)\) with \(H_{2}SO_{4}(aq)\) = \(-50\ kJ\ mol^{-1}\)
Standard enthaply changefor the reaction of Mg(s) with \(H_{2}SO_{4}(aq)\) = \(-467\ kJ\ mol^{-1}\)
Standard enthalpy change of formation of \(CO_{2}(g)\) = \(-394\ kJ\ mol^{-1}\)
Standard enthalpy change of formation of \(H_{2}O(l)\) = \(-286\ kJ\ mol^{-1}\)

questions.subject: Chemistry
topics: Topic 7: Redox Reactions, Chemical Cells and Electrolysis
questions.question_text:
Consider the experimental set-up shown below:

[IMG:q8_img_0.jpg]

sub_questions.label: a
sub_questions.question_text:
In the above experiment, the bulb lights up when the  \( SrBr_{2}(s) \)  becomes molten. 
(Atomic number of Sr = 38)

children.label: i
children.question_text: State the observation at carbon electrode X.

children.label: ii
children.question_text: Write a half equation for the change that occurs at carbon electrode Y.

sub_questions.label: b
sub_questions.question_text: Explain why the experiment should be performed in a fume cupboard.
sub_questions.answer: Bromine gas formed is toxic / poisonous.

sub_questions.label: c
sub_questions.question_text:
Zinc-carbon cells are used in the above experiment. The equation below shows the reaction that occurs in the zinc-carbon cells when the bulb lights up.

\( 2MnO_{2}(s)+2NH_{4}Cl(aq)+Zn(s)\rightarrow Mn_{2}O_{3}(s)+2NH_{3}(aq)+H_{2}O(l)+ZnCl_{2}(aq) \)

children.label: i
children.question_text: Deduce, in terms of change in oxidation number, the oxidising agent in a zinc-carbon cell.
children.answer:
Oxidation number of Mn decreases / changes from +4 to +3. 
Therefore  \( \mathrm{MnO}_{2}(\mathrm{s}) \)  is the oxidising agent.

children.label: ii
children.question_text: Write a half equation for the change that occurs at the cathode in a zinc-carbon cell.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question_text:
Three unlabelled reagent bottles each contains one of the white solids listed below :
\(  ZnSO_{4}\quad MgSO_{4}\quad MgSO_{4}\cdot7H_{2}O \)
Outline how you would carry out tests to distinguish these three solids.
questions.answer:
• Dissolve the solids separately in water.
• Add aqueous ammonia / NaOH(aq) to each of the solutions obtained until excess.
• White precipitate formed initially for all of them. But only the precipitate of  \( ZnSO_{4} \)  dissolves in excess aqueous ammonia / NaOH(aq).
- Heat respectively the two remaining solids in a test tube and place a piece of dry  \( CoCl_{2} \)  paper in the mouth of the tube.
- Only  \( MgSO_{4} \cdot 7H_{2}O \)  can turn dry  \( CoCl_{2} \)  paper from blue to pink.
• Communication mark

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question_text:
In an experiment, 2.0 mol of  \( \mathrm{SO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \)  and 2.0 mol of  \( \mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \)  are allowed to react in a closed container maintained at 950 K. The chemical equation for the reaction is shown below:

\( 2SO_{2}(g)+O_{2}(g)\rightleftharpoons2SO_{3}(g)\quad\Delta H=-198\ kJ\ mol^{-1} \)

When the reaction attains dynamic equilibrium, 1.8 mol of  \( \mathrm{SO}_{3}(\mathrm{g}) \)  is obtained.

sub_questions.label: a
sub_questions.question_text: What is meant by the term ‘dynamic equilibrium’?
sub_questions.answer:
At dynamic equilibrium, the rate of forward reaction is equal to the rate of backward reaction, and not equals zero. /
At dynamic equilibrium, reactants are converted to products and products are converted to reactants at equal rate. No net change is observed.

sub_questions.label: b
sub_questions.question_text: At 950 K, the equilibrium constant  \( K_{c} \)  for the above reaction is  \( 878 \, dm^{3} \, mol^{-1} \) . Calculate the volume of the container.

sub_questions.label: c
sub_questions.question_text: If the above equilibrium mixture is subjected to each of the following changes, will the number of moles of  \( SO_{3}(g) \)  obtained increase, decrease or remain unchanged? Explain your answer in each case.

children.label: i
children.question_text: increasing the temperature
children.answer: Decrease. The reaction is exothermic. Increase in temperature will cause the equilibrium position to shift to the left.

children.label: ii
children.question_text: adding a suitable catalyst
children.answer: No change. A catalyst will increase the rate of forward reaction and that of backward reaction to the same extent. / A catalyst has no effect on the equilibrium position.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question_text:
Under certain conditions, a pink compound X reacts with NaOH(aq) to give a colourless product. Three trials of an experiment were conducted to study the kinetics of the reaction. Firstly, three NaOH(aq) solutions were prepared by mixing different volumes of 2.0 M NaOH(aq) and \( H_{2}O(l) \) at \( 25^{\circ}C \) . After that, one drop of X was added to each of them and the time needed for the pink colour to disappear was recorded. The relevant data is shown below:
Volume of 2.0 M NaOH(aq) used / cm³Volume of H₂O(l) used / cm³Time needed for the pink colour to disappear / s
Trial 15.0061
Trial 24.01.076
Trial 33.02.0101

sub_questions.label: a
sub_questions.question_text: Why is it necessary to make the total volume of the reaction mixtures the same for the trials?
sub_questions.answer:
To ensure fair comparisons between the trials. /
To ensure the concentration of NaOH(aq) is the only variable. /
The volume of NaOH(aq) used can represent the concentration of NaOH(aq) in the reaction mixtures.

sub_questions.label: b
sub_questions.question_text: Given that at  \( 25^{\circ} \) C,  \( [H^{+}(aq)][OH^{-}(aq)] = 1.0 \times 10^{-14} \, mol^{2} \, dm^{-6} \) , calculate the pH of the NaOH(aq) solution prepared in Trial 2.

sub_questions.label: c
sub_questions.question_text: Based on the information provided, deduce one factor which affects the rate of this reaction.
sub_questions.answer:
The concentration of NaOH(aq)
The shorter the time for the pink colour disappeared, the faster the reaction. An increase in concentration of NaOH(aq) will result in an increase in the reaction rate.

sub_questions.label: d
sub_questions.question_text: Detection of colour change using naked eye is not accurate enough. Suggest an instrumental method that can be used to more accurately detect the colour change.
sub_questions.answer: Using colorimeter / measuring relative transmittance / absorbance of the mixture

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question_text:
Outline a synthetic route, with no more than three steps, to accomplish the following conversion. For each step, give the reagent(s), reaction conditions (as appropriate) and structure of the organic product.

[IMG:q12_img_0.jpg]

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question_text:
The structure of acetophenone is shown below :

[IMG:q13_img_0.jpg]

Heating a mixture of acetophenone and  \( NaBH_{4} \)  in methanol solvent under reflux can give two isomeric compounds P and Q. P and Q have the same melting point and same solubility in methanol.

sub_questions.label: a
sub_questions.question_text: Draw a labelled diagram of the set-up for heating the mixture under reflux.
sub_questions.answer: [IMG:ans_q13_0.jpg]

sub_questions.label: b
sub_questions.question_text: Suggest another reagent that can also react with acetophenone in a suitable solvent to give P and Q.
sub_questions.answer: \(LiAlH_{4} / H_{2} (catalyst - Pt)\)

sub_questions.label: c
sub_questions.question_text: What kind of isomers are P and Q?
sub_questions.answer: enantiomers / optical isomers

sub_questions.label: d
sub_questions.question_text: State one different physical property between P and Q.
sub_questions.answer:
optical activity /
P and Q rotate plane-polarised light to opposite directions to the same degree / extent.

sub_questions.label: e
sub_questions.question_text: Suggest a chemical test to show how acetophenone and P can be distinguished.
sub_questions.answer:
Correct chemical reagent
Correct observations with comparison between the tests on acetophenone and P

Possible tests and the corresponding observations:
\( Cr_{2}O_{7}^{2-}/H^{+} \)  acetophenone -- no change; P -- from orange to green
\( MnO_{4}^{-}/H^{+} \)  acetophenone -- no change; P -- from purple to colourless
\( MnO_{4}^{-} \)  acetophenone -- no change; P -- formation of brown ppt.
\( MnO_{4}^{-}/OH^{-} \)  acetophenone -- no change; P -- formation of brown ppt.
2,4-DNP acetophenone -- formation of orange ppt.; P -- no change
\( CH_{3}COOH/H^{+}/heat \)  acetophenone -- no change; P -- pleasant odur substance formed
2,4-DNP = 2,4-dinitrophenylhydrazine

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question_text:
Arrange sodium, aluminium, silicon and sulphur in decreasing order of electrical conductivity at room conditions, and explain your answer in terms of bonding and structure.

___
questions.answer:
Electrical conductivity: aluminium > sodium > silicon = sulphur (or: silicon > sulphur) 
Any 3 of the following items, each 1 mark
Both aluminium and sodium have giant metallic structures with delocalised electrons so that electrical conductivity of them is high.
The number of delocalised electrons of aluminium is more than that of sodium so that electrical conductivity of aluminium is higher than that of sodium.
Silicon has giant covalent structure and its electrons are not mobile and cannot conduct electricity. OR
Silicon has giant covalent structure and its electrons are not mobile. But silicon is a semi-metal and can conduct electricity in some conditions.
Suphur has simple molecular structure and its electrons are not mobile and cannot conduct electricity.
Communication mark
